title: practical english usage - Part 25
content:
After find + object, to be suggests the result of a test or investigation. Compare: 4 They      found       her    (to   be)  ...

For more details of structures with feel, ►    463; for know, ►    504; for think, ►    606.

Passive forms of these structures may be less formal than active forms (►    10.6).

I considered him  (to be) an excellent choice.

To be can be dropped after consider.

(More natural than I thought her to be mistaken.) I thought that she was mistaken.

This structure is very unusual with think.

(Less formal: They believed that she was reliable.) They believed her to be reliable.

(Less formal: We supposed that they were married.) We supposed them to be married.

(Less formal: I considered that he was an excellent choice.) I considered him  to be an excellent choice.

to be) in a formal style. ɪn an informal style, that-dauses are more common.

feel, know, find, understand) can be followed by object + infinitive (usually Some verbs that refer to thoughts, feelings and opinions (e.g. believe, consider, 3   I considered            him     to   be   ...

The police do not regard him  as (being) dangerous.

The  structure is also possible with as being.

After tests, they identified the metal as gold.

His mother regards him  as a genius.

She described her attacker as a tall dark man with a beard.

I see you as a basically kind person.

when we  say how  we  see or describe somebody/something.

After some verbs, an object complement is introduced by as. This is common 2  I see    you     as  ...

He painted red the walls, the window frames and the ceiling.

He painted the wall red. (     He painted red the wall.) A  complement may come  before a long and heavy object. Compare: 'Would you like to join the committee?' 7 would consider it an honour.' I don't know why they elected him  President.

I find her attitude strange.  Don't call me a liar.

You make me nervous. She's driving us crazy. Let's paint it blue.

This is often an adjective or noun phrase.

complement (an expression that gives more information about the object).

Some transitive verbs can be followed by an object together with an object 1  adjective       and   noun    complements me    nervous.

10 verb    + object     + complement:        You     make grammar •  11   linking verbs: be, seem, look, etc (►   463).

Other verbs used in two ways like this are look (►   518), taste (►  603) and feel not Isabel suddenly appeared in the doorway. (     ... sudden...) not The problem appeared impossible. (     ... impossibly.) verbs. They  are then used with adverbs, not adjectives. Compare: Some of these verbs are also used with other meanings as ordinary non-linking 3 other     uses himself - rather like saying He is intelligent. Seem is a linking verb.) He seems intelligent. (Intelligent is an adjective. It tells you about the person person spoke.) He spoke intelligently. (Intelligently is an adverb. It tells you about how  the After linking verbs we  use adjectives, not adverbs. Compare: 2 adjectives        after    linking     verbs That car looks fast.    It's getting late.

I do feel a fool.      The stew smells good.

The weather is horrible. She became a racehorse trainer.

examples: be, seem, appear, look, sound, smell, taste, feel, become, get.

These verbs can be called 'linking verbs', 'copulas’  or 'copular verbs’ . Common Some verbs are used to join an adjective or noun complement to a subject.

1  common       linking     verbs 11  lin  king    ve  rbs:  be,  seem,    loo   k,  et c ________ For the structures that are possible after a particular verb, see a good dictionary.

It was considered impossible to change the date.

Seven people are understood to have been injured in the explosion.

She was believed to belong to a revolutionary organisation.

For a long time he was thought to be a spy.

He is not regarded as being dangerous.

The metal was identified as gold.

Her attacker was described as a tall man with a beard.

It was painted blue. He was elected President.

Passive versions of these structures are common (►  65).
